Design rationale explains why specific trait bounds were chosen
README provides quick-start usage examples
Comparison with alternative designs that were considered and rejected
Well-designed generic API with compile-time safety guarantees
High-quality implementation following language idioms
Comprehensive test suite including property-based tests
Clear documentation of type-level design decisions

major_assignments.title: Language Interpreter: Multi-Paradigm Mini-Language
major_assignments.description: Design and implement an interpreter for a small programming language that supports at least three paradigms: first-class functions with closures, basic type checking (static or dynamic), and one concurrency primitive (async/await or channels). The interpreter must include a lexer, parser, AST representation, and evaluator. Students choose the language features but must justify their design trade-offs in a written report.
objectives: Build a complete lexer and recursive descent parser for a custom grammar
Implement an environment-based evaluator supporting closures and lexical scoping
Add type checking (static or dynamic) that catches type errors before or during execution
Implement at least one concurrency feature and demonstrate it in example programs
deliverables: Complete interpreter source code: lexer, parser, type checker, and evaluator
Formal grammar specification in BNF/EBNF for the language
Test suite with at least 30 test programs covering all language features
Example programs demonstrating closures, type safety, and concurrency
Design report (5-6 pages) explaining language design trade-offs and implementation choices

criteria: Lexer correctly tokenizes all language constructs
Parser produces correct AST for all valid programs
Error messages include source location and helpful context
Grammar handles operator precedence and associativity correctly
Closures correctly capture lexical scope
Variable scoping follows lexical (not dynamic) rules
Recursion works correctly including mutual recursion
Type checker catches type errors with clear messages
At least one concurrency primitive is fully functional
Example program demonstrates concurrent execution
Concurrency does not introduce crashes or data corruption
Design justifies the chosen concurrency model
Grammar is formally specified and consistent
Design trade-offs are discussed with reference to course concepts
Alternative designs considered and rejected with rationale
Example programs are non-trivial and demonstrate language expressiveness
Complete working interpreter with lexer, parser, and evaluator
Closures, type checking, and concurrency all functional
Comprehensive test suite covering all features
Thoughtful design report connecting to course concepts

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) testing conceptual understanding and coding ability

lab_assignments.description: Weekly lab exercises applying each paradigm in focused programming tasks

major_assignments.description: Three major projects: multi-paradigm pipeline, generic library, language interpreter

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam on functional programming, type systems, generics, and concurrency

final_project.description: Language interpreter capstone project presented in Week 15 with peer review

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Extensions granted only for documented emergencies.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of paradigm concepts and design approaches is encouraged. All code, analysis, and written work must be individual unless explicitly designated as group work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own. Using code from external sources requires attribution and is limited to utility functions, not core assignment logic. AI-generated code must be disclosed and understood.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python 3.12+, Rust toolchain (rustup, cargo), and Go 1.21+ installed. Lab computers are available with all environments configured. A personal laptop is recommended.
